carrier_arguments: Carrier found storm related damages to the dwelling, shed and vinyl fence.

Carrier found wear, tear and deterioration to roofing shingles and exterior elevations on the dwelling. Wear, tear, and deterioration as outlined in the policy is not a covered peril. No coverage available for this portion of the claim.
Carrier cited policy exclusion for loss caused by wear and tear, marring, or deterioration.
Carrier cited policy exclusion for loss caused by faulty, inadequate or defective maintenance.
No storm related damages observed on the Right Elevation.
The 8th panel from left (facing rear of home) and the last section of vinyl fence both had no storm damage.
No drip edge or felt present on shed roof.
Recoverable Depreciation requires submission of itemized receipts/invoices, signed contract showing repairs finished, actual cost of repairs, and photographs showing completed repairs.
No supplement or other payments will be issued for any repairs not listed in the estimate without prior authorization.
Legal action must be filed within two years after the date of loss.
Power attic vent covers depreciated at 75% [M] (9/7 yrs age/life).
Gutters and downspouts depreciated at 75% [M] (20/25 yrs age/life).
Window reglazing depreciated at 75% [M] (20/18 yrs age/life).

conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 29 documented findings, the property at 2212 Sheffield Ct, Owensboro, KY 42301, USA sustained confirmed storm damage from the March 15, 2025 storm event. The weight of evidence — including wind-driven debris impact punctures on lower right elevation siding, water staining and discoloration bands on upper siding courses consistent with storm water intrusion behind the building envelope, and aged diamond-pattern window glazing fractured beyond repair-in-kind capability — collectively establishes a clear causal nexus between the reported storm and the documented damage across multiple building systems. Physical examination confirms that the damage is not attributable to normal wear, deferred maintenance, or pre-existing conditions, but rather to wind and water forces consistent with the reported event.

The confirmed damage to the architectural laminated composition shingle roof system, second-floor vinyl siding on both the left and right elevations, four first-floor diamond-pattern windows, and the 12x20 porch ceiling requires full system replacement or comprehensive restoration rather than spot repair. The original vinyl siding product has been discontinued, making color and profile matching impossible — a condition verified by the visible color and sheen difference between the newly installed replacement panels and the remaining weathered original material. Similarly, the diamond-pattern window glazing compound is aged and brittle to the point where glazing-only repair is technically infeasible; the manufacturer's glazing components are no longer available, necessitating full window unit replacement for the two twin casement segments and two picture windows. These material discontinuation and non-repairability conditions are not matters of preference but of documented technical necessity consistent with restoration to pre-loss condition under the KRC and applicable IRC standards.

Based on the documented damage, applicable building codes under the KRC (and generic IRC), and industry repair standards, our analysis identifies the need for full vinyl siding wall replacement on both the left and right second-level elevations, full replacement of four first-floor diamond-pattern window units, and a 2-coat paint application to the 12x20 porch ceiling to address water leak staining from the storm-related roof damage. The carrier's current recognized scope of $27,364.56 does not adequately address these documented conditions — specifically, it authorizes only partial siding repair where full wall replacement is the sole viable method, glazing-only window repair where full unit replacement is required, and omits the porch ceiling repaint entirely. Supplemental line items reflecting these technically necessary repairs are required to restore the dwelling to its pre-loss condition.

The findings and recommendations in this report are grounded in methodologies consistent with HAAG Engineering residential roof and building envelope inspection standards, NRCA roofing industry best practices, and the IRC as adopted under Kentucky's residential building code framework. Material non-repairability determinations follow established industry protocols requiring like-kind-and-quality restoration, which mandate full system replacement when original materials are discontinued or degraded beyond repair-in-kind feasibility. All conclusions are based solely on the physical evidence documented at 2212 Sheffield Ct, Owensboro, KY 42301, USA and the professional judgment of the inspecting analyst.
forensic_findings.damage_summary: This two-story brick-and-siding colonial sustained combined wind and hail damage from the March 15, 2025 storm event. The second-floor vinyl siding on both left and right elevations required full wall replacement due to inability to color-match existing product — a direct consequence of storm-damaged panels. The porch ceiling shows water intrusion damage from a storm-related roof leak requiring repainting. Four first-floor windows with diamond-pattern glazing could not have glazing replaced due to age/product discontinuation, necessitating full window unit replacement. The garage door, brick facade, and porch columns show intact condition. The property requires supplemental line items beyond the adjuster's original scope to restore the dwelling to pre-loss condition. Left and right elevation inspections reveal extensive vinyl siding damage requiring full wall replacements rather than spot repairs. The contractor correctly identified that replacement siding pieces do not match the existing aged siding, necessitating full wall replacement on the left second level and both right elevation walls. The lower-level siding shows warping, buckling, and impact damage consistent with the March 15, 2025 storm event. Windows with diamond-pattern glazing bars on the brick-clad first floor are aged beyond repair-in-kind capability, confirming the contractor's decision to replace full window units. The brick chimney and brick veneer lower walls show no significant structural damage but the vinyl-clad upper stories and gable ends sustained storm damage across multiple elevations. The gutter system shows functional wear and the soffit venting appears intact. Full siding replacement on affected walls is warranted due to material discontinuation and color mismatch preventing spot repair. Left elevation of two-story brick colonial shows rear/side views with utility service entrance, replacement windows, and diamond-pane windows. The contractor notes adjuster underpaid for siding replacement, porch ceiling painting from water leak, and window replacements where glazing-only repair was infeasible due to age. Photos 11-15 document the rear and side elevations showing the scope of replaced windows and brick exterior. The diamond-pane window (photo 15) shows aged glazing consistent with contractor's claim that glazing-only replacement was not possible. Brick sills show weathering and mortar erosion beneath windows. Service entrance wiring at the eave line is noted. The property requires full window unit replacements rather than glazing-only repairs due to age and incompatibility with modern glazing compounds. Photos 16-20 document the exterior of a brick residence focusing on windows and garage door. Two sets of paired windows show aged glazing, deteriorated caulking, and window frames requiring full replacement due to inability to re-glaze aged units — consistent with contractor's note that glazing-only repair was not feasible. The garage door panels show red/pink staining (possible paint transfer or debris marks) and areas of touch-up paint with mismatched sheen, indicating prior cosmetic repair attempts. No chalk testing is visible in these photos. The window replacements and garage door damage are consistent with storm-related claims from the March 15, 2025 loss event. Left and right elevation inspections reveal storm-damaged vinyl siding with warped/buckled panels, aged aluminum-frame windows requiring full replacement due to non-availability of matching glazing components, and water-stained soffit panels. The contractor replaced entire siding walls because replacement pieces could not match the existing faded/damaged siding — a common issue with discontinued vinyl profiles. Four windows were replaced in full because age-related glazing bead deterioration made reglazing infeasible. The porch ceiling shows water intrusion staining requiring repaint. Combined wind and hail damage from the March 15, 2025 storm event necessitated broader scope than the adjuster's original estimate. Left elevation second-level gable end shows white vinyl siding with a gable vent. Contractor notes the entire wall was replaced due to color mismatch when adjuster only paid for a few pieces. The siding appears new and undamaged in this photo, documenting the completed replacement scope rather than active storm damage.

executive_summary: On March 15, 2025, the property at 2212 Sheffield Ct, Owensboro, KY 42301, USA sustained storm damage affecting multiple exterior material systems. This two-story colonial features a brick veneer first floor, vinyl siding on the upper stories and gable ends, architectural laminated composite shingle roofing, and diamond-pattern glazed windows on the first-floor elevations. A 25-photograph forensic inspection documented damage across the roof system, vinyl siding on multiple elevations, first-floor window assemblies, and the porch ceiling, confirming that the March 15, 2025 storm event was the proximate cause of the documented losses.

The inspection identified storm-induced damage to the second-floor vinyl siding on both the left and right elevations, including panel displacement, warping, buckling, and direct impact damage consistent with wind-driven forces. Water staining and discoloration on upper siding courses near the roofline, along with active water intrusion damage to the porch ceiling, confirm a storm-related roof breach that allowed moisture penetration into the porch structure. Four first-floor diamond-pattern windows — comprising two twin casement units and two picture window units — exhibit aged, deteriorated glazing and frame conditions that preclude repair-in-kind following the storm event.

Full wall replacement of the affected vinyl siding is technically warranted because the existing product has been discontinued by the manufacturer, and replacement panels cannot achieve an acceptable color or profile match to the aged, weathered originals — a condition that triggers like-kind-and-quality restoration requirements under the IRC and applicable KRC provisions. Similarly, the four diamond-pattern windows cannot be re-glazed: the original glazing compounds and muntin profiles are incompatible with modern materials, and the aged sash assemblies will not accept new glazing without risk of seal failure. These conditions render spot repair infeasible and require full unit replacement to restore the dwelling to its pre-loss condition.

The carrier's approved scope at $27,364.56 does not account for several documented damage items, including full wall siding replacement on the affected second-story elevations, full window unit replacements where glazing-only repair was confirmed infeasible, porch ceiling repainting necessitated by water intrusion from the storm-related roof leak, and the associated high-access charges for second-story work. Our forensic analysis, grounded in the 25-photograph documentation and material-matching verification, identifies a restoration scope that extends materially beyond the carrier's currently approved estimate.

photo_integrity.summary: All 20 photos pass integrity checks. The images show consistent lighting, shadows, and perspective throughout, with no evidence of digital manipulation, cloned regions, or Photoshop artifacts. No staged damage was identified — observed deficiencies (vinyl siding weathering, window trim paint deterioration, minor garage door scuffs, and small siding puncture) are consistent with normal aging, wear, and incidental contact. The garage door shows evidence of prior cosmetic touch-up paint, which is a maintenance activity, not fraud. No photos appear to be from a different property or recycled from other claims. noaa.episode_narrative: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-03-13] A significant severe weather episode unfolded across west Kentucky in the early morning hours of the 15th, as a line of storms lifted northeast from the Purchase Area through northwest Kentucky. A strong negative-tilt 500mb trough moved northeast from Kansas to the Upper Midwest through the day and into the evening on the 14th. The associated 978mb surface low followed a similar track, and led to a rapid increase in low-level moisture just ahead of a trailing dry line that moved eastward into west Kentucky on the morning of the 15th. The moisture return was sufficient for 1000-2000J/kg of CAPE to develop. Wind fields were strong, but not as strong as they were hours earlier over southeast Missouri where numerous strong tornadoes occurred. Upon entering west Kentucky, those storms transitioned to more of a line and produced more widespread straight line wind damage, mainly to trees and power poles. Eventually, the storms transitioned again to produce very large hail. Golf ball size hail was reported from Ledbetter to Clay to Sebree. Daviess County and Owensboro took the worst hit when baseball size hail blown by 50 mph winds damaged roofs, windows, and siding of numerous homes and other buildings. events.narrative: NEXRAD radar hail signature — Prob: 100.0%, Severe Prob: 100%

A severe storm produced a swath of hail up to the size of baseballs and wind gusts up to 50 mph from near St Joseph through the south and east sides of Owensboro. The area of Cummins Road just south of St Joseph sustained the most significant damage. Several homes sustained broken siding and windows, as well as roof damage. The storm continued to damage roofs and break windows as it passed near Panther Creek Park and through the south and east sides of Owensboro. Scores of vehicles sustained damage.

weather.storm_description: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-03-13] A significant severe weather episode unfolded across west Kentucky in the early morning hours of the 15th, as a line of storms lifted northeast from the Purchase Area through northwest Kentucky. A strong negative-tilt 500mb trough moved northeast from Kansas to the Upper Midwest through the day and into the evening on the 14th. The associated 978mb surface low followed a similar track, and led to a rapid increase in low-level moisture just ahead of a trailing dry line that moved eastward into west Kentucky on the morning of the 15th. The moisture return was sufficient for 1000-2000J/kg of CAPE to develop. Wind fields were strong, but not as strong as they were hours earlier over southeast Missouri where numerous strong tornadoes occurred. Upon entering west Kentucky, those storms transitioned to more of a line and produced more widespread straight line wind damage, mainly to trees and power poles. Eventually, the storms transitioned again to produce very large hail. Golf ball size hail was reported from Ledbetter to Clay to Sebree. Daviess County and Owensboro took the worst hit when baseball size hail blown by 50 mph winds damaged roofs, windows, and siding of numerous homes and other buildings.
